text:
HEPATOPANCREATOBILIARY ONCOLOGY — MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD CONSULT NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Hepatopancreatobiliary Medical Oncology
SERVICE: Gastrointestinal Oncology / HPB Tumor Board
NOTE TYPE: New Patient Consultation / Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 66-year-old retired pipe fitter presenting for multidisciplinary tumor board evaluation and oncologic consultation for newly diagnosed borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. He was referred following completion of staging workup including endoscopic ultrasound with FNA, pancreatic protocol CT, PET/CT, and molecular profiling.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski came to medical attention approximately 3–4 weeks ago with a several-week history of progressive jaundice, epigastric discomfort radiating to the mid-back, and unintentional weight loss estimated at 5–6 kg over the preceding 2 months. He also reported new-onset greasy, light-colored stools and mild pruritus, consistent with obstructive biliary physiology. He denied hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. Review of prior records does not identify antecedent abdominal imaging that would have captured an earlier lesion.

Initial laboratory evaluation revealed a total bilirubin of 2.1 mg/dL, direct bilirubin elevated, and serum CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL. CEA was mildly elevated at 4.8 ng/mL. Liver enzymes were mildly elevated with a cholestatic pattern. An outpatient abdominal ultrasound prompted cross-sectional imaging, which revealed a 3.8 cm hypovascular mass in the head of the pancreas with upstream main pancreatic duct dilation to 6 mm and mild common bile duct dilation.

The patient subsequently underwent pancreatic protocol CT (triphasic CT abdomen/pelvis), which demonstrated the 3.8 cm ill-defined hypovascular mass in the pancreatic head with abutment of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120–150 degrees of circumference and short-segment abutment/encasement of the SMV-portal vein confluence without occlusion. Two periportal lymph nodes measuring up to 1.2 cm were noted, suspicious for nodal involvement. There was no hepatic or peritoneal metastatic disease.

Whole-body FDG PET/CT confirmed a hypermetabolic primary pancreatic head mass (SUVmax 8.4) with mildly FDG-avid periportal lymph nodes (SUVmax 3.1 and 2.8). No distant metastatic disease was identified.

Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with fine needle aspiration of the pancreatic head mass was performed and yielded diagnostic material. Cytopathology confirmed adenocarcinoma consistent with pancreatic ductal origin, with malignant glandular cells showing nuclear pleomorphism, irregular nuclear membranes, and prominent nucleoli. Immunohistochemistry on the cell block demonstrated CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, MUC1 positive, MUC2 negative — a profile consistent with pancreaticobiliary origin. Diagnosis: MALIGNANT — ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreas.

Next-generation sequencing (NGS) was performed on the EUS-FNA specimen. Relevant findings include: KRAS p.G12V mutation (VAF 42%), TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 38%), SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion. Tumor mutational burden was 3.2 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). No homologous recombination deficiency detected (BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2 all wild-type). No actionable fusions detected. PD-L1 CPS 1 (low). In summary, this is a KRAS G12V-driven, MSS, HRD-negative pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma with no currently actionable targeted therapy beyond standard cytotoxic chemotherapy.

Given biliary obstruction with a total bilirubin of 2.1 mg/dL, the patient underwent ERCP with placement of a plastic biliary stent, which was performed without complication. Bilirubin has trended toward improvement post-stenting. The case is now being presented to the HPB multidisciplinary tumor board for treatment planning.

Overall disease staging: Stage III, cT3N1M0 per AJCC 8th edition. Per NCCN/AHPBA criteria, the tumor is classified as BORDERLINE RESECTABLE based on SMA abutment < 180 degrees and short-segment SMV/PV involvement.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — managed with metformin; glycemic control described as fair
2. Hypertension — medically managed
3. Tobacco use disorder — remote; 30 pack-year history; quit 15 years prior
4. No prior pancreatic surgery
5. No prior abdominal malignancy
6. No history of pancreatitis or chronic pancreatic disease

---

SURGICAL HISTORY:
- ERCP with plastic biliary stent placement (recent, for biliary decompression prior to this visit)
- No other prior abdominal surgeries

---

FAMILY HISTORY:
- No known family history of pancreatic cancer
- No known family history of BRCA1/BRCA2-associated cancers (breast, ovarian, prostate)
- Father: coronary artery disease
- Mother: type 2 diabetes mellitus

---

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Mr. Kowalski is a retired pipe fitter. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and quit 15 years prior to this presentation. He denies current tobacco use. Alcohol use: occasional (1–2 drinks per week, less now given illness). No illicit drug use. He lives with his wife and reports a strong home support system. He is ambulatory and independent in activities of daily living. He has Medicare insurance coverage. He does not report occupational chemical exposures that are clearly documented as carcinogenic for pancreatic cancer, though asbestos and chlorinated solvent exposure are plausible in the context of his occupation as a pipe fitter.

---

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies

---

MEDICATIONS AT TIME OF CONSULTATION:
1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (for type 2 diabetes mellitus)
2. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily (for hypertension)
3. Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily (for hypertension)
4. Aspirin 81 mg orally daily
5. Ursodiol (per gastroenterology post-stenting)
6. Multivitamin daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Progressive jaundice (improving post-biliary stenting), back pain (mid-epigastric radiating to mid-back), weight loss (~5–6 kg over 2 months), decreased appetite, early satiety, fatigue, light-colored/greasy stools (steatorrhea), mild pruritus (resolving).
Negative: No fever or chills. No hematemesis. No melena or hematochezia. No nausea or vomiting at rest. No new neurological symptoms. No chest pain or shortness of breath. No lower extremity edema. No dysuria.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Alert and oriented x3. Mildly cachectic-appearing male in no acute distress. Mild residual scleral icterus, improved compared to pre-stenting reports.
Vitals: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, Heart rate 74 bpm, Temperature 37.0°C, Respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 79 kg (BMI 26.2). Weight 5–6 kg below reported usual weight.
HEENT: Mild residual scleral icterus bilaterally. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular chains. No Virchow's node palpated.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft. Mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable abdominal mass. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No shifting dullness. Bowel sounds normoactive. No Sister Mary Joseph nodule palpated.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness. No skin changes.
Skin: Mild residual jaundice. No spider angiomata.
Neurological: Alert, oriented, appropriate. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No gross motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal.
Lymph nodes: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy.
Performance Status: ECOG performance status 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out light work).

---

PERTINENT LABORATORY RESULTS:
CA 19-9: 1200 U/mL (markedly elevated; normal < 37 U/mL)
CEA: 4.8 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
Total bilirubin: 2.1 mg/dL (improving post-stenting; direct bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL)
ALT: 62 U/L (mildly elevated, cholestatic pattern)
AST: 48 U/L (mildly elevated)
Alkaline phosphatase: 218 U/L (elevated)
GGT: 310 U/L (elevated)
Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with nutritional compromise)
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL (normal; eGFR > 60 mL/min/1.73m2)
Hemoglobin: 12.4 g/dL (mild anemia, likely anemia of chronic disease/nutritional)
Platelets: 278 x 10^9/L (normal)
ANC: 4.1 x 10^9/L (normal)
HbA1c: 7.8% (fair glycemic control)
INR: 1.1 (normal)

---

IMAGING REVIEWED:

1. CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Pancreatic Protocol (triphasic): 3.8 cm ill-defined hypovascular pancreatic head mass with upstream main pancreatic duct dilation (6 mm) and mild common bile duct dilation. SMA abutment estimated at 120–150 degrees. Short-segment SMV/portal vein confluence involvement without occlusion. Two periportal lymph nodes up to 1.2 cm, suspicious for malignant involvement. No hepatic metastases. No peritoneal disease. No ascites.

2. FDG PET/CT Whole Body: Hypermetabolic pancreatic head primary (SUVmax 8.4). Mildly FDG-avid periportal nodes (SUVmax 3.1, 2.8). No distant metastatic disease. No peritoneal carcinomatosis confirmed.

3. EUS: Hypoechoic 3.8 cm mass in pancreatic head with ductal involvement. Vascular involvement consistent with CT findings. EUS-FNA performed.

---

PATHOLOGY / MOLECULAR REVIEW:
- EUS-FNA Cytology: Adenocarcinoma, consistent with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. CK7+, CK20−, CDX2−, MUC1+.
- NGS: KRAS G12V (VAF 42%), TP53 R248W (VAF 38%), SMAD4 homozygous deletion, CDKN2A homozygous deletion. TMB low (3.2 mut/Mb). MSS. HRD-negative. No BRCA1/2, ATM, PALB2 mutations. No actionable fusions. PD-L1 CPS 1.

---

ASSESSMENT:

Mr. Kowalski is a 66-year-old male with newly diagnosed Stage III borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head of the pancreas, cT3N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition). The tumor is 3.8 cm with SMA abutment of approximately 120–150 degrees and short-segment SMV/portal vein involvement, meeting NCCN criteria for borderline resectability. CA 19-9 is markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL. Molecular profile reveals KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 deletion, and CDKN2A deletion — a high-risk molecular signature with no currently actionable targeted therapy (specifically, not a KRAS G12C mutation and therefore not eligible for sotorasib or adagrasib). MSS status and low TMB preclude benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors. BRCA2/ATM wild-type status indicates no PARP inhibitor indication.

Biliary decompression has been performed via ERCP plastic stent. The patient has ECOG PS 1 with adequate organ function and performance to tolerate aggressive cytotoxic regimens.

The case was presented in full detail at the HPB multidisciplinary tumor board today. In attendance were HPB surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, interventional radiology, gastroenterology, diagnostic radiology, and pathology.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION SUMMARY:

The board reviewed all available imaging (CT pancreatic protocol, PET/CT), pathology (EUS-FNA cytology), molecular profiling (NGS), and relevant laboratory data. There was consensus that primary upfront surgical resection is not recommended given the degree of vascular involvement — specifically SMA abutment and SMV/portal vein contact — which would carry a high risk for positive margins (R1 or R2 resection). The surgical team concurs that primary resection at this time would be technically challenging and carries significant risk of incomplete resection.

The board consensus recommendation is neoadjuvant chemotherapy with the goal of downsizing the tumor, improving the vascular relationships, and converting this patient to a state more favorable for surgical resection with clear margins. Based on the published evidence from the PRODIGE 24/CCTG PA.6 trial demonstrating superiority of modified FOLFIRINOX over gemcitabine in metastatic disease, and extrapolation of this to the neoadjuvant setting supported by multiple institutional series and the Alliance A021501 trial experience, the board recommends neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy.

Plan: FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles (approximately 4 months), with restaging CT pancreatic protocol after cycle 4 and after cycle 8, and reassessment of resectability at completion of chemotherapy. If good response with improved vascular anatomy, the plan will be to consider consolidative chemoradiation followed by reassessment for Whipple procedure.

Germline genetic testing: BRCA1/2 and ATM germline testing ordered today per NCCN guidelines for all patients with pancreatic adenocarcinoma, regardless of family history. Results pending.

Regarding diabetes: Metformin to be held on chemotherapy infusion days (days 1–2 of each cycle) given IV contrast use and risk of lactic acidosis; otherwise to be continued. Glycemic management to be optimized throughout treatment.

---

PLAN:

1. NEOADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY — FOLFIRINOX:
   - Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours, Day 1
   - Irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV over 90 minutes, Day 1
   - Leucovorin (folinic acid) 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours, Day 1 (concurrent with irinotecan)
   - 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus, then 2400 mg/m2 IV over 46 hours continuous infusion
   - Repeat every 14 days (q2w); target 8 cycles total
   - GCSF support: Pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) 6 mg subcutaneously on Day 2 of each cycle for primary prophylaxis (per institutional protocol for patients at high risk of febrile neutropenia given age and FOLFIRINOX-associated myelosuppression)
   - Antiemetic prophylaxis: Ondansetron 8 mg IV plus dexamethasone 12 mg IV pre-chemotherapy on Day 1; prochlorperazine 10 mg PO q6h PRN breakthrough nausea
   - Irinotecan-related cholinergic prophylaxis: Atropine 0.25–0.5 mg IV/SQ for acute cholinergic symptoms; loperamide 4 mg PO at first loose stool, then 2 mg q2h during waking hours for delayed diarrhea (max 16 mg/day)
   - Metformin: hold on chemotherapy days (Day 1 and Day 2) as noted above; restart Day 3
   - Dose reduction guidelines: planned dose reductions per institutional protocol for grade 3–4 hematologic or non-hematologic toxicities

2. BILIARY STENT MANAGEMENT:
   - Plastic biliary stent in place; gastroenterology to schedule stent exchange in approximately 8 weeks to prevent stent occlusion
   - Monitor bilirubin monthly; hepatobiliary panel at each visit

3. RESTAGING:
   - CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol after cycle 4 (approximately 2 months)
   - CA 19-9 before each cycle
   - Repeat comprehensive restaging CT after cycle 8, followed by tumor board reassessment for resectability

4. GERMLINE TESTING:
   - BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM (and PALB2) germline testing: blood draw ordered today; genetic counseling referral placed
   - Results expected within 3–4 weeks

5. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT:
   - Dietitian referral placed — significant weight loss (5–6 kg) and likely evolving pancreatic exocrine insufficiency
   - Consider initiation of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT, e.g., Creon) if steatorrhea persists or worsens; will reassess at first follow-up
   - High-calorie, high-protein oral supplements recommended

6. DIABETES MANAGEMENT:
   - Continue metformin with modification per chemotherapy days as above
   - HbA1c 7.8%; endocrinology co-management to be considered if glycemic control worsens during chemotherapy

7. FOLLOW-UP:
   - First chemotherapy cycle to be initiated within the next 5–7 days pending patient readiness and port placement
   - Port-a-cath placement scheduled through interventional radiology (required for 46-hour 5-FU infusion)
   - Return to medical oncology clinic on Day 14 (prior to cycle 2) for toxicity assessment, labs (CBC, CMP, CA 19-9)

8. PATIENT EDUCATION:
   - Extensive counseling provided to patient and wife regarding diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment plan
   - Goals of neoadjuvant therapy discussed: to shrink the tumor, improve the relationship with blood vessels, and achieve a situation where complete surgical removal may be possible — with the ultimate goal of resection and potential for cure or prolonged disease-free survival
   - Risks and expected side effects of FOLFIRINOX discussed in detail including: neuropathy, myelosuppression (neutropenia), fatigue, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, alopecia, mucositis
   - Patient and his wife verbalized understanding. Patient expressed desire to pursue all appropriate treatment options and is agreeable to the neoadjuvant plan.

Baseline CA 19-9: 1200 U/mL (to be used as marker of treatment response).
ECOG PS: 1.

—
Note dictated by HPB Medical Oncology Attending, reviewed and attested.
Case discussed at HPB Multidisciplinary Tumor Board with representation from HPB surgery, medical oncology, radiation oncology, gastroenterology, diagnostic radiology, and pathology.
